title: The Value of Central Clearing
abstract: I study a contracting innovation that suddenly insulated traders of hedging contracts against counterparty risk: central clearing counterparties (CCPs) for derivatives. The first CCP was created in Le Havre (France) in 1882, in the coffee futures market. Using triple difference-in-differences estimation, I show that central clearing changed the geography of trade flows Europe-wide, to the benefit of Le Havre. Inspecting the mechanism using trader-level data, I find that the CCP solved both a “missing market” problem and adverse selection issues. Central clearing also facilitated entry of new traders in the market. The successful contracting innovation quickly spread to other exchanges.

article.introduction:
Financial frictions can prevent gainsfrom trade from being realized and thus resources from being efficiently allocated. However, whether financial frictions impair trade is not exogenous: agents can design contracts to mitigate or undo their effects (Allen and Gale (1988)). Throughout history, a number of contracting innovations—from the bill of exchange to limited liability—improved capital allocation and risk-sharing, thus contributing to long-term growth (North (1991)). Yet empirically identifying the causal impact of contracting innovations on resource allocation is challenging. In particular, it is often difficult to precisely date the appearance of contracting forms that are now widespread in financial markets, and data to study these events are often lacking.
In this paper, I study a contracting innovation that has become essential in present-day financial markets, namely, central clearing counterparties (CCPs) for derivatives. As an experiment, I use the creation of theCaisse de Liquidation des Affaires en Marchandises(CLAM) in the market for coffee futures in Le Havre (France) in 1882. The contracting innovation brought about by the CLAM is to fully insulate futures traders against counterparty risk. Specifically, for the first time, a clearing institution interposed itself between traders, becoming a buyer to any seller and a seller to any buyer, so as to guarantee the execution of contracts. Before 1882, earlier clearinghouses, for example, in Liverpool or New York, only offered netting services to facilitate the settlement of transactions. The contracting arrangement invented by coffee traders in Le Havre quickly spread worldwide and, after the 2008 financial crisis, central clearing became mandatory globally for standardized derivatives.
I hand-collect rich archive data and conduct empirical analysis that yields two contributions. The first is to show that greater risk-sharing opportunities with derivatives had a significant causal impact on trade flows in underlying commodities. The creation of the CLAM arguably satisfies exogeneity requirements, as it did not follow any pre-trend in trade flows in Le Havre, and it was heavily debated by traders who did not foresee its impact on trade flows. For identification, I estimate a triple difference-in-differences model. The three sources of variation in trade flows that I exploit are (i) before and after 1882, (ii) between Le Havre and other European markets where clearing is not introduced and, (iii) within Le Havre, between coffee and other commodities that remain uncleared. The specification enables many confounding factors, such as commodity-specific and harbor-specific demand and supply shocks, to be ruled out.
The estimation results show a positive and significant effect of the introduction of central clearing on coffee trade flows in Le Havre. I first estimate the triple difference-in-differences model within France, comparing trade flows in Le Havre with 22 other harbors. I find that the share of coffee imports entering France in Le Havre increased by 11.1 percentage points relative to control commodities. I address residual endogeneity concerns by replicating the estimation with cotton trade flows. Although the CLAM was created to mitigate counterparty risk on coffee futures, it also accepted clearing of cotton futures. In this case, the creation of the CLAM was exogenous to conditions in the cotton market. Yet the results continue to hold when focusing on cotton trade flows, using other textiles as a control.
Next, I extend my analysis to the European scale, reconstructing trade flows in cleared and control commodities for seven countries. I show that central clearing changed the geography of trade flows Europe-wide: after 1882, a significantly larger share of coffee entered Europe in Le Havre, was warehoused there, and eventually reexported to neighboring countries. The share of coffee imports from France by the sample countries increased by 2.9 to 7.3 percentage points, relative to control commodities. I also provide suggestive evidence that larger inventories of coffee in Le Havre helped smoothing coffee consumption in France. Finally, I support the quantitative results above with narrative evidence. Many contemporaries wrote that the CLAM boosted trade in Le Havre and, within 10 years, nine other European futures exchanges had introduced CCPs. Therefore, the contracting innovation spread quickly. The focus on trade flows distinguishes the paper from earlier work by Bernstein, Hughson, and Weidenmier (2019), who study the impact of clearing on the pricing of counterparty risk.
As a second contribution, I pin down the mechanism through which central clearing affected trade flows. The main claim is that central clearing increased the ability of coffee dealers to hedge their inventories. This in turn induced them to import more coffee, warehouse it until it is demanded for consumption, and re-export it. For this mechanism to explain the findings, several requirements must be met. Most importantly, it must be the case that hedging markets dysfunction before central clearing, and restart afterward. Consistent with this idea, I show that central clearing was introduced following a global crisis in coffee markets in 1880, during which counterparty risk impaired hedging possibilities. I then show that the CLAM was explicitly designed to mitigate counterparty risk, with high equity, high margin requirements, and a chairman of the board with a strong reputation for honesty. A last requirement for the improvement in hedging technology to explain the results is that traders make use of the CCP. Remarkably, even though use of the CLAM was costly and not mandatory, an overwhelming majority of traders were using it.
I then distinguish between two channels through which hedging possibilities could increase. A first possibility is that, before central clearing, traders were willing to hedge against the default risk of their counterparties, but instruments to do so were missing. Under this argument, the CCP solved the “missing market” problem and thus made derivatives better hedging instruments. To test for this channel, I study changes in the composition of the pool of established traders around the introduction of the CLAM, using trader-level data on coffee transactions. If the main friction before central clearing is the existence of a missing market, then trading should concentrate among traders with a low observable probability of default. Consistent with this idea, I find that the share of low-quality traders, defined as traders exiting the market within the next two years, was low before the CLAM (8.2%). Furthermore, it did not increase with its creation, while trading volume in futures increased massively (by 303% within six months). Thus, for established traders, a missing market problem was preventing hedging ex ante.
A second possibility is that central clearing mitigated an adverse selection problem: before the CLAM was in place, traders were unable to distinguish between high- and low-quality counterparties, which impaired futures trading. I provide several pieces of evidence consistent with adverse selection being significant for a subset of traders. To begin, there is evidence that the system that prevailed before central clearing, under which futures trading was uncollateralized and based on reputation, dysfunctioned during the 1880 crisis: it became harder to distinguish between high- and low-quality traders based solely on reputation. Traders designed a CCP to separate trader types by requiring costly collateral to be posted. In this context, central clearing was the most cost-efficient mechanism to require collateral, due to its netting benefits. Additionally, I provide econometric evidence that adverse selection was significant for a subset of traders. Indeed, I show that after central clearing was in place, the share of new traders (defined as traders who entered the market in the preceding two years) increased, as did entry in the coffee market. I also show that trader turnover decreased after central clearing, in line with the prediction that the quality of the pool of traders should increase if the CCP induced a separation of types. Overall, central clearing helped distinguish between trader types and removed informational barriers to entry in the futures market for traders with no ex ante reputation.
The findings on both channels raise an additional question about the creation of the CLAM. Central clearing was introduced by established traders, for which adverse selection may not have been large. By doing so, established traders facilitated entry and made markets more competitive. I argue that this apparent tension between incumbents and new entrants can be rationalized using the theory by Duffie, Dworczak, and Zhu (2017), developed in the context of benchmark prices: by introducing central clearing, incumbents lost informational rents (arising from their established reputation) to entrants, but were better off ex post because trading volume increased massively. Consistent with this view, I find no subsequent decrease in creditworthiness for incumbents that created the CLAM.
This paper is related primarily to the literature studying how the design of contracts and institutions can enable gains from trade to be realized (North and Thomas (1973), Allen and Gale (1988), Greif (2006)). A number of contracting innovations throughout history fostered long-term growth, including insurance mechanisms (De Roover (1945)) or bills of exchange and payment clearing (Börner and Hatfield (2017)). I show that central clearing is contracting innovation that fostered long-distance trade. Relatedly, while the relationship between finance and the allocation of resources has been intensely studied (see Levine (2005) for an overview), to my knowledge this paper is the first to show a causal relationship between the ability to hedge and trade flows.
This paper is also linked to the growing literature on central clearing. Existing papers study CCP design (Koeppl, Monnet, and Temzelides (2012), Biais, Heider, and Hoerova (2016)), netting (Duffie and Zhu (2011)), collateralization (Duffie, Scheicher, and Vuillemey (2015), Menkveld (2017), Cruz Lopez et al. (2017)), defaults (Bignon and Vuillemey (2020)), and the ability of CCPs to mitigate fire sales (Vuillemey (2020)). The paper closest to the current study is Bernstein, Hughson, and Weidenmier (2019), who show a reduction in counterparty risk premia in the U.S. equity market after the establishment of the NYSE clearinghouse in 1892. The main difference between the two papers is that I do not focus on the effect of central clearing on the pricing of cleared derivatives, but rather on trade flows in underlying assets.
The paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, I introduce the theory and the main hypothesis. In SectionII, I provide historical background and describe the data, while I test hypotheses related to trade flows in SectionIII. I explore the mechanisms in SectionIV. Finally, SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Theory and Hypothesis:
In several asset classes, trading requires dealers to hold nonzero inventories for a period of time. When dealers' ability to hold inventories is limited, due either to risk-aversion or adverse selection, they agree to buy large asset quantities only at a price discount (Glosten and Milgrom (1985), Grossman and Miller (1988)). Thus, relative to an efficient benchmark, financial frictions prevent gains from trade from being realized. If, instead, dealers could costlessly hedge price risk, this would induce them to hold larger inventories and trade volume would increase.1
Indeed, if dealers can better hedge their inventories after central clearing is in place, more gains from trade can be realized. Although I test Hypothesis 1 in the context of physical commodities, it could also hold for financial securities.
Two distinct mechanisms can explain why central clearing is a contracting innovation that allows for more efficient hedging. First, it may be the case that, before central clearing, the friction impairing market functioning is the lack of an insurance market to trade counterparty default risk (Biais, Heider, and Hoerova (2012)). If so, dealers can hedge their inventories with futures, but these futures may not always pay off when desired, due to residual counterparty risk. Central clearing then solves a missing market problem: by insulating traders against counterparty risk, it makes derivatives better risk-sharing instruments.
Second, it may be the case that hedging markets dysfunction before central clearing due to adverse selection problems. If traders are unable to distinguish between low- and high-quality counterparties (i.e., those with high or low probability of default), they agree to trade only at a price discount. Anticipating this outcome, high-quality traders self-select out of the market (Akerlof (1970), Gorton and Pennacchi (1990)). In this context, central clearing expands the set of hedging possibilities by inducing a separation of trader types: only traders able to post sufficient collateral can clear trades with the CCP. The collateral required by the CCP plays a screening role (Bester (1987)), and the ability to hedge subsequently increases.2
To distinguish between these two channels, theory makes distinct predictions about the composition of the pool of traders around the introduction of central clearing. If the main friction is a missing market, but there is no asymmetric information, then trading before central clearing should be concentrated among traders with a low observable probability of default. When a CCP is in place, the quality of the pool of traders should then weakly decrease: either traders with high default risk can enter the market (since counterparties no longer worry about default risk), or they are kept away from the market by the CCP, for example, via high margin calls. Instead, if adverse selection is significant ex ante, the fraction of low-quality traders (those with a high unobservable probability of default) should drop with central clearing. Indeed, after a CCP is in place, high-quality traders re-enter the market as price discounts for counterparty risk disappear. A related prediction is that new traders—for which information asymmetry concerns are arguably larger—also become more prevalent after central clearing. To summarize, while the missing market channel predicts that the average quality of the pool of traders decreases with central clearing, the adverse selection channel predicts that it increases.
article.II. Historical Background and Data:
In this section, I describe the creation of the CLAM, its functioning, and the data used in the analysis.
In the second half of the 19thcentury, the north western part of Europe was the most active trade area globally. Both free-trade policies and technological progress (steamboats replacing sailboats) boosted intra-European and transatlantic trade. Harbors such as Antwerp, Hamburg, Le Havre, Liverpool, and Rotterdam were intensely competing to attract trade flows. Le Havre was the largest French harbor in this “Northern range,” and the second largest harbor in France, after Marseille.
The market for physical coffee had one economically important characteristic. Although coffee was produced in distant countries, primarily Brazil, it was not stored in these countries. After the crops, coffee was exported to European harbors to be warehoused. Then, whenever demand for consumption in a region rose, short-distance boats would cater to it from warehouses. The time gap between production and consumption could extend up to one year, until the next crop. As a result, coffee dealers had to hold large inventories of physical coffee for possibly long periods of time, giving rise to significant price risk. The ability of a harbor to attract trade flows thus depended critically on the risk-bearing capacity of local dealers.3
To hedge the price risk associated with inventories of physical commodities, forward sales and purchases had existed for centuries. Starting in the 1860s, the standardization of contracts led to the creation of organized futures exchanges in several harbors. In a few of them, such as Liverpool, clearinghouses were created to centralize the settlement of transactions. However, these institutions did not insulate traders against counterparty risk. In Le Havre and other European cities, futures transactions were uncollateralized. The reputation of a trading house was the main guarantee against counterparty risk and a substitute for collateral. Trading was thus concentrated among well-established trading houses linked to notable families.
The first CCP insulating traders from counterparty risk was created following a crisis in the global coffee markets in 1880 to 1881 (see Internet Appendix Section II.A for details).4Amid rising fears of counterparty risk due to falling coffee prices, the reputation of trading houses was no longer sufficient to support trade. In Le Havre, a group of well-established coffee traders assembled to create theCaisse de Liquidation des Affaires en Marchandises(CLAM) as a limited liability corporation.5The CLAM started operating on December 16, 1882. Compared to preexisting clearinghouses, the contracting innovation was that the CCP would substitute itself to original parties in a transaction, thereby insulating traders against bilateral default risk.6
Remarkably, in most respects the functioning of the CLAM was similar to that of modern CCPs. After two traders agree on a transaction, they report it to the CLAM, which becomes counterparty to each of them. Subsequently, the CLAM eases the settlement of trades, via multilateral netting, and insulates each trader against the default of its initial counterparty. The CLAM employs two main instruments to protect itself against the default of traders. First, each counterparty must be a broker domiciled in Le Havre, which is akin to a membership requirement. Second, the CLAM calls variation margins each day from traders realizing losses (based on changes in their position's market value). Variation margins must be received within one day. Furthermore, at the inception of any trade, an initial margin is paid, which protects the CCP in the event that a trader defaults on its variation margins. When a trader defaults on margin calls, the CLAM seeks to immediately liquidate its position, to return to a matched book. Potential losses arising from the liquidation of a trader's position are first borne by the trader's initial margins.7If initial margins are insufficient, residual losses are borne by the CCP's equity. This last point is the main difference with modern CCPs: the CLAM does not have a default fund or other loss-sharing mechanism between surviving members (“default waterfall”). Finally, the CLAM earns revenues from proportional fees on each cleared transaction.
I use three main data sets. First, I use the archive sources described in Internet Appendix Section I to reconstruct the history and functioning of the CLAM. This includes early rulebooks, annual reports, board minutes, legal acts containing exhaustive lists of equityholders, and a number of descriptive accounts by contemporaries about the CLAM's operations. Second, I obtain data about futures trading activity, I collect data on future prices and quantities traded, as well as daily data on the identity of traders in physical coffee, from a daily newspaper published by Le Havre's exchange, theBulletin de Correspondance. I additionally retrieve yearly data on the identity of commodity traders, from the registerAlmanach du Commerce du Havre, and on their creditworthiness, from the archives of the Banque de France. Third, for my main tests, I collect yearly data on trade flows in coffee and other relevant commodities for France and seven other European countries over the period 1876 to 1889. These data are obtained separately from each country's customs statistics (see Internet Appendix Section I.B for detailed sources).
article.III. Central Clearing and Trade Flows:
In this section, I show that the reduction in counterparty risk in Le Havre's futures market led to a significant change in trade flows Europe-wide.
To assess the effect of central clearing on trade flows, I use the introduction of the CLAM as an experiment and estimate triple difference-in-differences models. Identification of a causal effect relies on exogeneity requirements: central clearing should be as good as randomly assigned, with respect to both treated harbors and treated commodities. My setting is characterized by several features such that these exogeneity requirements are arguably satisfied.
Regarding the treated harbor, the CLAM was a response to heightened counterparty risk in Le Havre. However, there is no evidence that risk was higher in Le Havre than in other markets. For example, Internet Appendix Section II.A shows that it was much lower than in New York, where all three of the largest trading houses had failed. Relatedly, central clearing was not introduced at a time when Le Havre became more or less attractive for trade flows in general. As can be seen in Panel A of Figure1, total imports and exports in Le Havre, in terms of either volume or value, do not exhibit a specific trend before the period 1882 to 1883. Furthermore, central clearing was a heavily debated innovation, and evidence shows that traders had no clear understanding of what its effects on trade flows would be. For example, Depitre (1907, p. 162) writes that “At the beginning, opinions were strongly divided in the commercial circles in Le Havre. A number of important trading houses refused to participate in the CLAM and several of them avoided any relationship with it.”8Similarly, the costs and benefits of central clearing remained debated abroad for years. In the New York Coffee Exchange, the introduction of central clearing was debated by the Board of Managers multiple times but never adopted (Emery (1896)). In Hamburg, the commodity exchange was long convinced that the costs of a CCP outweigh its benefits (Fuchs (1891)). In this context, it is unlikely that traders in Le Havre adopted central clearing because of preexisting knowledge that it would boost trade flows.
Regarding the treated commodity, the CLAM was explicitly set up to mitigate counterparty risk in the market for coffee futures. However, from the very beginning, it also accepted other commodities for clearing, primarily cotton. In the case of cotton, remaining concerns about the endogeneity of the treatment are thus absent. In SectionIII.C, I show that the results obtained for coffee trade flows hold equally well when focusing on cotton trade flows. Below, I also show evidence for parallel trends between coffee and other commodities before 1882.
I first estimate equation (1) on the full set of 23 French harbors over the period 1877 to 1888 (i.e., six years before and after the treatment).11The reason for these sample boundaries is that, in 1888, a CCP for coffee futures (created in 1887) became operational in Hamburg, which could have confounding effects. As control commodities, I use the so-called “colonial commodities.” This corresponds to the commodity group in which coffee is classified in the customs data, and includes cocoa, pepper, sugar, tea, and tobacco. For identification, one advantage of this setup is that these commodities are not produced on European soil, in which case changes in trade flows cannot reflect changes in home production. TableIreports the share of French imports by commodity arriving in the main harbors in the 1877 to 1882 period preceding the creation of the CLAM. As can be seen, Le Havre was by far the most important harbor for coffee trade flows (60.5% of total imports), and also a large market for tobacco (34.9%), cocoa (32%), and pepper (21.2%).
The regression estimates are displayed in TableII. The baseline estimate, in column (1), shows that, relative to the control commodities, the share of coffee imports arriving in Le Havre increased by 11.1% (significant at the 1% level) after the creation of the CLAM. The magnitude of the effect is economically large. Compared to a preclearing share of 60.5%, this represents an increase in coffee imports for Le Havre of 18.3%. This effect is illustrated graphically in Panel B of Figure1, which plots the yearly share of France's coffee imports in Le Havre compared to the weighted average share of imports of other commodities. This figure additionally shows fairly parallel trends between coffee and the control commodities before 1882.
The baseline regression result is robust to alternative definitions of the control group. In column (2), I reestimate the baseline specification after excluding sugar from the control group, since sugar is also produced in Europe. The magnitude of the effect remains of similar magnitude (9.2% vs. 11.1%) and equally significant. In column (3), the control group is the sum of all imports at the harbor level. The estimated effect of introducing the CLAM (a 17.7-percentage-point increase in the share of coffee) is even larger than in the baseline specification. Finally, in column (4), I estimate the same regression as in column (3) but with exports as the dependent variable. The estimated effect is larger than for imports, as the share of Le Havre's coffee exports within France's exports increases by 24.7 percentage points. Therefore, the regression results consistently indicate that the introduction of central clearing had a large positive effect on trade flows within France. In Internet Appendix, I additionally show that the triple-difference estimates are robust to alternative definitions of the sample period (Internet Appendix Section III.A) and cannot be explained by changes in shipping technology (Internet Appendix Section III.B).
Although large, the economic magnitude of the estimated effect is realistic. Indeed, as discussed in SectionII.A, coffee imported to Europe was not consumed immediately, but warehoused and potentially re-exported within Europe. This storage activity exposed dealers to price risk. The ability to hedge price risk was thus a key determinant of the place in which commodities were warehoused. Consequently, the creation of the CLAM, which increased hedging ability, led to a change in the geography of imports—not because more coffee was consumed in Le Havre, but because more stocks were held in Le Havre. This can be seen from column (5) of TableII, which reestimates equation (1) using the share of French stocks for each commodity as the dependent variable. I find that the share of French coffee stocks in Le Havre increased by 15 percentage points after 1882, relative to the stocks of other commodities.
A potential endogeneity concern is that unobserved characteristics simultaneously led to the adoption of clearing in the coffee market and made coffee trade more attractive in Le Havre around 1882. To rule out this concern, I systematically read local newspapers in 1882. I do not find any mention of omitted factors. To further address this concern, I construct a formal test. Notably, while the CLAM was created to address issues in the coffee market, other commodities—primarily cotton—also became centrally cleared.12The introduction of clearing in the cotton market is thus fully exogenous to conditions in the cotton market.
With the above in mind, I reestimate the triple difference-in-differences model in equation (1) with either raw cotton or the sum of raw cotton and cotton textiles as treated commodities. The control group includes either raw wool or the sum of raw wool, wool textiles, and silk textiles. The estimation results, reported in columns (1) and (2) of TableIII, show a positive and significant effect of central clearing on cotton imports relative to other textile imports. The magnitude of the effect is similar to that obtained for coffee, with an increase in the share of cotton imports between 7.2% and 14.2% depending on the specification. The endogeneity of the treatment is thus unlikely to be a major concern in the coffee trade regressions.
The same test can be reproduced in subsequent years, when additional commodities became eligible for clearing. One of the first commodities to be added was indigo, in 1887. In column (3) of TableIII, I reestimate equation (1) using indigo as the treated commodity and the sum of all imports as the control, over the 1882 to 1893 window. I again find a positive and significant effect of central clearing on imports. Since the event date in this case is not the same as for coffee and cotton, this test alleviates the concern that previous results were driven by factors specifically related to these commodities in 1882.
The estimation results, in TableV, show that central clearing had a significant effect on trade flows Europe-wide. All European countries in the sample increase the share of their coffee imports from France after the CLAM is in place, with this increase significant in all cases except for Italy.15Interestingly, the magnitude of the effect is relatively similar across countries, ranging from 2.9 percentage points for Hamburg to 7.3 percentage points for Germany. Economically, these magnitudes are large. Indeed, as seen in TableIV, the pre-treatment import shares from France were between 2% and 3% in several cases (Hamburg, The Netherlands, Norway, Sweden). The estimates therefore imply that, for these countries, imports from France increased two- to threefold. In Germany, where the pre-treatment import share for coffee was only 0.9%, the effect is even larger. These results are consistent with the idea that, while more coffee was warehoused in Le Havre after the creation of the CLAM, a significant part was re-exported to other European countries. Access to better hedging instruments thus reinforced the role of Le Havre as a hub for coffee trade in Europe. This result is illustrated in Figure2, which compares the average share of coffee that European countries import from France (weighted by trade volume) to the weighted average share of control commodities that they import from France. We see parallel trends until the creation of the CLAM, afterward we see a divergence in coffee trade flows.
The economic magnitude of the effect can be used to approximate the private value of central clearing to traders. Assuming that the only costs to traders of deviating trade to Le Havre are transportation costs, average freight rates for intra-European routes is informative about the resources traders are willing to forgo to access central clearing. Based on freight rates provided by Angier (1920), which I convert into French francs and compare to coffee prices, transportation costs for intra-European routes represented between 1.5% and 2.5% of coffee prices in the 1880s.16This represents a sizable fraction of the price that traders are forgoing. However, not all trade flows were redirected to Le Havre, and it is likely that routes with cheaper freight rates were more likely to face reallocation. The estimates in TablesIIandVsuggest that between 5% and 10% of total European trade flows (for the sample countries) were redirected to Le Havre after 1882.17Therefore, in monetary terms, an upper bound for the private value of central clearing for all European traders corresponds to 0.25% of the total value of European coffee imports.
The econometric evidence finds support from narrative evidence. Indeed, several contemporaries noted the positive impact of central clearing on coffee trade flows in Le Havre. In front of a parliamentary commission on futures markets in 1897, coffee dealers stated that “since 1880, imports have increased so much that, within a few years, Le Havre has become the largest market globally.”18Ten years later, Depitre (1907) wrote that “it is not disputable that the creation of the CLAM significantly contributed to maintain the preponderance and to foster the commercial development of the harbor of Le Havre.” Interestingly, foreign commentators reached a similar conclusion. In the United States, Emery (1898) wrote that “it is well-known that the establishment of future trading in Le Havre in 1881 greatly increased the coffee business of that port at the expense of Hamburg.” Similar statements can be found in German sources.19
Indirect evidence that central clearing was beneficial to trade activity in Le Havre can also be obtained by studying the reaction of competing harbors. When it became clear that the increase in trade in Le Havre was attributable to the CLAM, other European markets introduced CCPs. Within 10 years, nine European exchanges had CCPs in place: Paris (1885, 1887), Antwerp (1887), Hamburg (1887), Amsterdam (1888), Marseille (1888), Magdeburg (1889), Reims (1890), Leipzig (1890), and Roubaix-Tourcoing (1892). In cases where documentation is available, it is clear that the adoption of central clearing was motivated by the successful experiment in Le Havre.20The contracting innovation therefore spread quickly to other markets.21
The findings so far show that central clearing enabled dealers in Le Havre to hold significantly larger inventories of physical coffee. I now provide suggestive evidence that these inventories insulated local consumption against global supply shocks. Theoretically, the volume of local inventories can help smooth local consumption, relative to other countries, if a friction prevents perfect reallocation of coffee stocks across countries. Although frictions preventing reallocation were arguably low in calm times—giving rise to intense intra-European trade flows—large price swings associated with high levels of counterparty risk could temporarily disrupt cross-country reallocation. To provide empirical evidence on this mechanism, the sample period considered offers a good laboratory in the form of a large negative supply shock at a global scale, namely, bad crops in Brazil in years 1886 to 1887. I use this shock to study cross-country differences in the response of coffee consumption.
The shock I study is displayed in Figure3. As can be seen, coffee prices boomed by 140% within a few months in the 1886 to 1887 period. This large supply shock has several appealing features. First, as required by theory, there is evidence that the shock was associated with elevated levels of counterparty risk in futures markets where no CCP was in place.22Second, the shock occurred following the creation of the CLAM but before the creation of CCPs in other European markets. Third, while food consumption is typically hard to measure, coffee consumption can be precisely assessed because there is no home production of coffee in Europe. I obtain yearly data on coffee consumption in 11 European countries from the U.S. Department of Agriculture (Graham (1912)).
The main result can be seen in Figure4. Although world coffee consumption fell by 17.6% between 1886 and 1887, European consumption (excluding France) fell by 16.6%, German consumption by 17.7%, and French consumption by only 6.0%. I next focus more precisely on cross-country differences within Europe, reporting data for 11 European countries in TableVI. This table shows that France observed the smallest drop in coffee consumption between 1885 and 1887. The fact that France exhibits smoother coffee consumption can also be seen when separately examining the main drop (1886 to 1887, in column (2)) and the rebound (1887 to 1888, in column (3)).23Finally, while all French harbors reduced coffee stocks in 1886 and 1887, Le Havre's stocks decreased by 64.4%, from 73 million tons in December 1885 to 26 million tons in December 1887. Overall, these results are consistent with the idea that larger local inventories insulated local consumption against supply shocks. That said, these results are less cleanly identified than the results based on trade flows, as comprehensive consumption data are not available for other commodities in the control group.
article.IV. Mechanism:
In this section, I explore the mechanism driving the effect of central clearing on trade flows. I find that central clearing improved hedging possibilities by mitigating both missing market and adverse selection problems.
I start by providing evidence supporting the view that the increase in trade flows is due to increased hedging possibilities in the futures market. For this to be true, three requirements must be met: (i) frictions preventing the smooth functioning of hedging markets before the introduction of central clearing must be severe enough to materially impair trade, (ii) the CCP must be designed to significantly reduce the frictions that impair hedging, and (iii) investors must use the CCP to a large extent. In the case of the CLAM, all three requirements are indeed met.
Concerning (i), the CLAM was created following a long period of high counterparty risk. As discussed in Internet Appendix Section II.A, abundant narrative evidence indicates that global coffee markets could not function efficiently in 1880 to 1881, following a large drop in coffee prices. Figure5further shows that the number of active commodity traders decreased between 1880 and 1883, consistent with counterparty risk being high. In addition, in Le Havre, traders increased coffee stocks dramatically in 1881 to 1882, betting on a price reversal. As they became cash-constrained, counterparty risk for potential financiers was large. Sayous (1898) reports that “as the future looked highly uncertain, one needed [...] to find a way to reduce the risks for capitalists.”
With respect to (ii), several pieces of evidence indicate that the founders of the CLAM explicitly sought to create a credible and robust institution to fully eliminate counterparty risk in futures. First, the CLAM was established with the support of several reputable families, as discussed in Internet Appendix Section II.B. As Depitre (1907, p. 162) notes, “the founders chose, as chairman of the board, W. Iselin, a highly reputed trader, from an excellent and very old trading house importing coffee, and notoriously staying away from speculative deals.” In addition, only 25% of the equity (2,000,000 FRF) was initially paid-in, so as to retain a callable buffer of 75%. The CLAM also started operations with stringent collateral requirements, which were deemed excessive by some members but were not lowered. At the founding general assembly (December 8, 1882), several participants complained about the magnitude of the initial margins. According to the minutes, “Mr. Le Normand [one of the founders] answers that the company is above all a financial company: the members of the assembly are shareholders before being brokers or dealers; one should consider the general interest of the company, not the particular interest of each shareholder; if initial margins are lower, the decrease may be advantageous for some counterparties, but disadvantageous for the company and its shareholders.” It is also notable that, in subsequent years, the CLAM was never at risk of defaulting on its commitments.
In support of requirement (iii), there is evidence that virtually all traders soon used the CLAM, in spite of early opposition by some trading houses. This is remarkable given that there was no requirement to centrally clear transactions executed in the exchange. For example, De Rousiers (1903, p. 172) wrote that “No law, no local rule require anyone to register futures transactions at the CLAM; however, all serious businessmen in Le Havre use it. Through their daily actions, for more than 20 years, they give a strong support to the CLAM, they recognize that its discipline is not uselessly tough.” Furthermore, according to Depitre (1907), even traders who explicitly wanted to avoid the CLAM after its creation were using it after just a few months. The fact that the CLAM was widely used is also evident from its early and high profitability, discussed in Internet Appendix Section II.C. For example, the CLAM was already able to pay dividends in 1883.
The fact that the CLAM was successful at boosting hedging activities can be seen directly by studying trading volume, collected at a daily frequency from theBulletin de Correspondance. Figure6shows a marked increase in hedging activities around the introduction of central clearing. Between the six months preceding the creation of the CLAM and the six months following, trading volume in coffee futures increased by 303% on average.
A related question is whether the CLAM, in addition to boosting the quantity of futures traded, had an impact on asset prices. In Internet Appendix Section IV, I compare the level and volatility of coffee prices in Le Havre and New York using difference-in-differences estimation over a period of 18 weeks before and after the creation of the CLAM. I find no significant short-term impact of central clearing on coffee prices, regardless of the dependent variable used. There can be two reasons for this result. First, testing the impact of central clearing on asset prices is more difficult in futures markets than in the equity market studied by Bernstein, Hughson, and Weidenmier (2019). Indeed, futures are in zero net supply and counterparty risk can be material on both the buyer's and the seller's side, in which case prices can go up or down depending on which side experiences more material counterparty risk. Second, traders may respond to counterparty risk in futures markets by exiting the market rather than by pricing risk.24This could explain why central clearing has a large impact on the volume of hedging activities, but no measurable impact on prices.
I now try to distinguish between two mechanisms through which central clearing could improve hedging possibilities: the missing market channel and the adverse selection channel discussed in SectionI. A first possibility is that, before central clearing, traders were willing to hedge against the default risk of their counterparties, but instruments to do so were missing. Although the missing market problem may not have been large historically, it could have become more severe with the 1880 crisis. Indeed, each traders' probability of default arguably increased. If this channel explains the findings, then the main prediction is that trading before central clearing is concentrated among agents with the lowest observable probability of default, and the quality of the pool of traders decreases after central clearing. If, instead, even the highest quality incumbents face severe adverse selection before central clearing is in place, then the pool of traders before central clearing should include a significant number of low-quality traders, but their number should decrease after central clearing.
To test these predictions, I would ideally use trader-level data on futures transactions, but such data are not available. Instead, I collect daily data on the names of individual traders in the spot coffee market from theBulletin de Correspondance. In doing so, I rely on the assumption that, in the cross-section of traders, greater access to futures markets positively correlates with access to the spot market. I collect these data for a period covering one year before and after the creation of the CLAM. Furthermore, for each trader I find the year in which they started and ceased operations from yearly registers of commodity traders (Almanach du Commerce du Havre).25Panel A of TableVIIdescribes the data using two-samplet-tests comparing the number and concentration of traders around the creation of the CLAM. The average number of traders increased at both the daily and the monthly frequency after the CLAM was in place (by 0.7 and 3.7 traders, respectively), while the concentration of trading activity—as measured by the Herfindhal index—decreased.
In this section, I examine whether the CLAM also mitigated adverse selection problems for a different subset of traders. Narrative evidence based on accounts by contemporaries suggest that this was partially the case, and that the creation of the CLAM can be interpreted as follows. Until 1880, futures trading was based on reputation—well-established families had been in the coffee business for generations, had a locally well-known name, and were highly visible in town.26Reputation was thus instrumental to separate high- and low-quality traders, that is, traders with a low versus high probability of default. This is consistent with the idea that reputation can substitute for collateral to mitigate adverse selection problems (Diamond (1989)). During the 1880 crisis, however, the reputation of at least some traders was weakened, and became insufficient to support trade, as discussed in Internet Appendix Section II.A. This can be interpreted as a temporary situation of partial pooling of high- and low-quality traders. In this context, central clearing can be seen as a mechanism to re-separate trader types, based on the explicit collection of costly collateral (Bester (1987)). Narrative evidence is consistent with the view that central clearing induces a separation of trader types. For example, De Rousiers (1903, p. 172) wrote that “very few of the critiques would dare engaging in a bilateral transaction that would not be centrally cleared.” More generally, some traders complained that they were unable to find counterparties for uncleared trades because their low quality was then revealed.
Whether adverse selection was a cause of market dysfunction can be tested formally, based on two predictions. First, traders with no preexisting reputation should be excluded from the market before central clearing is in place, but likely to enter afterward. To see whether this is the case, I reestimate equation (3) using the share of new traders as the dependent variable, where new traders are defined as traders who entered the market in the preceding two years. The results, reported in Panel B of TableVII, show that the share of new traders jumps significantly after central clearing is in place. In terms of magnitude, this share rises by a factor of three, from 4.8% to 14.7%. This result is consistent with the idea that new traders, whose reputations are not yet established, faced significant adverse selection problems. To further test this idea, I collect trader identities from yearly registers until 1886. As can be seen in Figure5, there was significant entry of new commodity traders after 1883. Among them, those explicitly identified as coffee traders increased from 43 in 1882 to 55 in 1886.27In sum, adverse selection created informational barriers to entry before the CLAM was in place, and central clearing relaxed these barriers by mitigating adverse selection problems.
If central clearing solves adverse selection problems, a second prediction is that the composition of the pool of traders should be tilted more toward high-quality traders after the CCP is in place, so that default rates should decrease. Although default rates are not observed, I use data from the yearly registers to compute yearly turnover rates, that is, the fraction of traders operating in yeart−1who no longer operate in yeart. Figure7plots trader turnover from 1875 to 1889, together with the average turnover levels before and after the creation of the CLAM. As can be seen, the turnover rate drops from above 10% to about 8% after the introduction of central clearing, consistent with an improvement in the quality of the pool of traders. That said, this result is suggestive: it cannot be given a causal interpretation because it could be driven by time effects (e.g., better macroeconomic conditions starting in 1883).
Finally, the argument that the CLAM mitigated adverse selection critically relies on the fact that collateral requirements play a screening role. However, a question that arises is whether traders could have mitigated this friction via bilateral collateralization. In the context considered here, evidence suggests that the cost of posting cash or high-quality securities as collateral was high. In addition to being cash-constrained, importers faced transaction costs associated with bilateral margin calls: posting collateral required visiting every counterparty to deliver and collect securities (which were paper certificates). Compared to this situation, the creation of a CCP reduced the cost of posting collateral. First, all collateral was posted in one place.28Second, and more importantly, by becoming the direct counterparty to every transaction, the CLAM could offer multilateral netting facilities, that is, call margins based on the net position of any given trader across all of the traders' counterparties. And third, there was no risk that the CLAM would abscond with collateral. In sum, creating a CCP was more cost-efficient than collateralization of bilateral futures to induce separation of high- and low-quality traders.
The coexistence of two distinct mechanisms explaining the effect of central clearing raises a question about the incentive structure that led to the creation of the CLAM. As discussed in Internet Appendix Section II.B, the CCP was created by established traders, for which adverse selection was not large. By introducing central clearing to mitigate a missing market problem, established traders reduced informational barriers to entry for new traders and made the coffee futures market more competitive. There are two interpretations of this tension between established and new traders. First, it may be the case that established traders did not foresee the effects of central clearing on entry and competition. Second, and more interestingly, it may be the case that established traders benefited from the CLAM even though they subsequently lost part of their reputational rents due to higher competition. According to this second interpretation, the introduction of central clearing could be understood along the lines of the model by Duffie, Dworczak, and Zhu (2017), who study incentives by dealers to introduce benchmark prices in opaque markets. In their model, dealers can voluntarily agree to make over-the-counter markets more transparent by introducing benchmarks if the increase in trading volume more than offsets the loss of informational rents.
In the context of the CLAM, three pieces of evidence support an interpretation as in Duffie, Dworczak, and Zhu (2017). First, as Figure6shows, trading volume in futures increased considerably after central clearing was in place, by 303% within six months. Second, based on yearly reports by the Banque de France on the creditworthiness of traders, there is no systematic evidence that incumbents' creditworthiness decreased after the creation of the CLAM (see Internet Appendix Section II.D). Third, there is no evidence that the CLAM's equityholders subsequently tried to restrict access to the market. For example, in 1885 the CLAM widely opened its base of equityholders to incorporate new traders (see Internet Appendix Section II.B).
In this section, I rule out alternative mechanisms that could explain my results.
Price transparency: A first possibility is that the trade results that I document are due to an increase in price transparency associated with the creation of the CLAM. As Internet Appendix Figure IA.1 shows, public price information shifted from an approximative discussion of prices (left panel) to a detailed price schedule for each maturity (right panel) on the day the CLAM opened. Theoretically, price transparency should reduce price dispersion and informational rents, increase matching efficiency, and allow more gains from trade to be realized (Duffie, Dworczak, and Zhu (2017), Martin (2018)).
This mechanism is unlikely to dominate here. First, while it is true that price transparency improved, it did not do so only for traders in Le Havre. In the 1880s, prices were quickly disseminated by telegraph to all other European markets and to the United States.29Thus, if price transparency improved in all markets, we should not expect the trade effect to be localized in Le Havre. Second, the futures exchange and the CLAM were separate entities, so that one could trade futures and choose not to clear them with the CLAM. Thus, if traders had been primarily seeking price transparency, they could have engaged in free-riding by benefiting from price information while avoiding the costs associated with using the CLAM. Instead, as discussed, virtually all traders cleared trades through the CLAM, and the CLAM earned significant profits from clearing fees in the first year of operation.
Standardization: A second possible alternative mechanism to explain the trade effects above could be the increase in contract standardization that coincided with the creation of the CLAM. A futures market was created in Le Havre in 1881, slightly more than a year before the creation of the CLAM. At that time, futures became more standardized. Standardization further increased with the creation of the CLAM, as one single coffee grade became publicly quoted. Standardization makes contracts more liquid. Indeed, due to the existence of externalities in liquidity provision decisions, a single traded future is more liquid than multiple contracts (Pagano (1989)). Moreover, if all investors trade standardized grades, the concern that some investors have private information specific to a given grade is lower. Therefore, bid-ask spreads, which compensate for adverse selection, are lower (Gorton and Pennacchi (1993)) and risk-sharing is easier.
This alternative explanation can be ruled out based on arguments already described above. Most importantly, if investors were primarily seeking standardized contracts regardless of whether they are centrally cleared, they could free ride on the CLAM. This is inconsistent with the fact that virtually all transactions were centrally cleared. Second, while the use of the CLAM was restricted to counterparties located in Le Havre, trading was open to investors in other markets. Therefore, if the availability of more standardized contracts was a dominant force, there is no reason why the effect should be concentrated in Le Havre.
Why is the effect localized?: Although the first two alternative mechanisms are inconsistent with a localized effect in Le Havre (since price transparency and standardization increased for virtually everyone), several factors suggest that access to central clearing was easier for traders located in Le Havre. Indeed, whiletradingwas open to any investor,clearingwas easier for traders located in Le Havre. First, any counterparty to the CLAM had to be located in Le Havre. Counterparties located outside Le Havre could therefore clear trades only via a broker. Moreover, the ability to pay margin calls was more limited for counterparties outside Le Havre, for technological reasons. Finally, the CLAM could refuse to register transactions if it lacked information about counterparties, which was more likely to occur for distant counterparties. It is therefore is reasonable to conclude that the creation of the CLAM insulated traders from counterparty risk to a greater extent if they were based in Le Havre.
article.V. Conclusion:
In this paper, I study central clearing, a contracting innovation that suddenly insulated traders of hedging contracts against counterparty risk. After its introduction for coffee futures in Le Havre in 1882, central clearing significantly changed the geography of trade flows in France and Europe: for cleared commodities, trade flows increasingly went through Le Havre. Regarding the mechanism, central clearing both resolved a missing market problem and mitigated adverse selection issues.
Although central clearing proved beneficial when first introduced, one should take care when drawing implications for current debates about mandatory central clearing for at least two reasons. First, central clearing in the 1880s was voluntary. Mandatory clearing may create new agency problems, since CCPs now have more captive clients and potentially benefit from implicit guarantees. Second, the CLAM closely resembled a member-owned institution. In contrast, for-profit CCPs, which are now common, may be less inclined to manage risk so as to avoid externalities that impair the market's functioning. Thus, more research is needed on the optimal design of CCPs when regulation is in place.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
